name: Questions (क्या / कहाँ / कब)
summary: Form yes/no questions with क्या and information questions with कहाँ, कब, कौन, and more.
explanation:
There are two kinds of questions in Hindi. A yes/no question is made by putting क्या (kya) at the very start of a statement: आप हिंदी बोलते हैं (aap Hindi bolte hain, you speak Hindi) becomes क्या आप हिंदी बोलते हैं? (kya aap Hindi bolte hain?, do you speak Hindi?). At the start of a sentence, this क्या simply turns a statement into a yes/no question; you can even drop it and just raise your intonation.

Information questions use a question word, most of which begin with the sound 'k': क्या (kya, what), कहाँ (kahaan, where), कब (kab, when), कौन (kaun, who), क्यों (kyon, why), कैसे (kaise, how), and कितना (kitna, how much/many). Notice that क्या means both 'what' and the yes/no marker; its position tells you which: at the start it is the yes/no marker, in the middle it means 'what', as in यह क्या है? (yeh kya hai?, what is this?).

Word order is otherwise the same as a statement, with the verb at the end. The question word usually sits where the answer would go: आप कहाँ रहते हैं? (aap kahaan rahte hain?, where do you live?) puts कहाँ in the slot the place name would fill in the answer.
pattern: yes/no: kya + statement; info: question word in the answer's slot + verb at end

conjugation.क्या (kya): what / yes-no marker - यह क्या है? (yeh kya hai?)
conjugation.कहाँ (kahaan): where - आप कहाँ हैं? (aap kahaan hain?)
conjugation.कब (kab): when - तुम कब आओगे? (tum kab aaoge?)
conjugation.कौन (kaun): who - वह कौन है? (voh kaun hai?)
conjugation.क्यों (kyon): why - तुम क्यों रो रहे हो? (tum kyon ro rahe ho?)
conjugation.कितना (kitna): how much / many - यह कितना है? (yeh kitna hai?)

examples.native: क्या आप तैयार हैं? (kya aap taiyaar hain?)

examples.en: What is this? (kya means 'what' in the middle)

examples.native: वह आदमी कौन है? (voh aadmi kaun hai?)
आप यह क्यों कर रहे हैं? (aap yeh kyon kar rahe hain?)

commonMistakes: Putting क्या (kya) in the middle when you mean a yes/no question; the marker goes at the very start.
Adding 'do' or 'does' as a separate word; Hindi has no auxiliary like English.
Changing the word order dramatically; questions keep the verb at the end like statements.
Confusing कौन (kaun, who) with कहाँ (kahaan, where), which sound and look similar.
Forgetting that the answer word's position is where the question word usually goes.
tip: Most Hindi question words start with 'k'. Learn the family kya, kahaan, kab, kaun, kyon, kaise, kitna together as a set.
